topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my phone screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My stomach clenched, a familiar, tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I scrolled down, my thumb hovering over the screen, feeling a strange mixture of shock and a deep, sinking shame. He had tagged me in a photo from the school event—a candid shot of me laughing, something I hadn't even realized he’d captured. Suddenly, every casual interaction felt magnified, scrutinized. I reread the message, the words seeming to mock my recent, careless posting. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over me, making my throat feel dry and tight. I quickly minimized the app, shoving the phone face down on the desk, the silence of my room suddenly deafening.

[story 2]

I found myself constantly checking my feed, a compulsive habit I couldn't shake. It was Mr. Davies. His profile picture, usually composed and academic, was now beside a picture of me from the science fair. The way his name appeared in the notification bar sent a jolt of heat through my chest, quickly followed by a cold, heavy dread. I watched the little 'like' counter on his recent posts, noticing the subtle shift in his engagement, the way he seemed to be paying closer attention to my online presence. When I finally dared to open his profile, the sheer proximity of his digital life to mine felt suffocating. My hands started trembling slightly as I navigated away, wanting nothing more than to disappear into the anonymity of the dark web, away from this unwanted visibility.

[story 3]

The realization hit me during lunch. I was scrolling through my feed, mindlessly consuming content, when I saw it: Mr. Chen had shared a story update. It was a picture taken outside the library, and there, clearly visible, was me, looking slightly distracted. A profound sense of foolishness settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I felt my cheeks flush a deep crimson, a heat rising from my neck to my hairline. I avoided looking up, focusing intently on my sandwich, the sound of my peers’ chatter becoming a distant, irritating buzz. Every time I glanced towards the common area, I felt exposed, as if a spotlight had been deliberately aimed at my every move, an undeniable feeling of having misjudged the boundaries of what was appropriate.
